text: Passengers who have queries or wish to make a complaint regarding Assisted Travel can do so via the 'Queries/Feedback' link.

Ryanair recognizes the sunflower lanyard as a subtle way for passengers with hidden disabilities to indicate they may require extra assistance. Lanyards are available at reception areas and special assistance points at all participating airports within the Ryanair network.
Further information regarding the Hidden Disabilities Sunflower can be found on their official website.

Passengers uncertain about their fitness to fly are recommended to contact their doctor. Ryanair requires medical clearance in specific circumstances, including recent illness, hospitalization, injury, surgery, or long-standing conditions that necessitate special services.
Passengers with medical conditions such as breathing difficulties or heart disease are advised to consult their doctor before making a flight reservation.

Passengers traveling with a broken limb in a cast must notify Ryanair of their condition and any assistance requirements. Seat requirements are as follows: one seat is required for upper limb casts (waist and above) or lower leg casts (below the knee); three seats must be purchased for a full leg cast to allow for elevation and reduce swelling. Additionally, any cast fitted for less than 48 hours must be split along its entire length.

Passengers must be able to perform the following tasks when instructed by the crew: fasten and unfasten their seatbelt, retrieve and fit a lifejacket unaided, don an oxygen mask unaided, and understand the safety briefing and emergency instructions.

Passengers who require assistance with feeding, toileting, or administering their own medicines and medical procedures must travel with a personal assistant. Failure to do so may result in denial of boarding if the carriage is reasonably believed to pose a safety risk.
Each non-disabled accompanying adult can assist a maximum of one reduced mobility passenger and must purchase a standard adult fare.
Passengers who have pre-booked assistance requirements should arrive at the boarding gate at least 30 minutes prior to the flight departure.

To use an accepted postural aid onboard, passengers must contact the airline at least 48 hours before departure. It is recommended to add postural support at the time of booking to ensure a suitable seat is allocated free of charge.
Postural support aids are only accepted for onboard use if they have been approved by a recognized aviation authority, such as EASA, IAA, UK CAA, or FAA.
For reduced mobility passengers traveling with an accompanying adult, the airline will make every effort to ensure the accompanying passenger is seated next to them.
To receive pre-booked special assistance, passengers must present themselves at the airport's special assistance desk at least 2 hours before their flight departure.

Certain child restraints and postural supports are accepted for onboard use in addition to the aircraft safety belt, subject to safety restrictions. Ryanair does not provide these items; they must be supplied by the passenger.

Ryanair uses the following codes to determine the level of assistance required for passengers: 
- WCHR: Assistance through the airport to the boarding gate (departure and arrival).
- WCHC: Assistance through the airport and lift on/off to/from the aircraft seat (departure and arrival).
- WCHS: Assistance through the airport and up/down the aircraft steps (departure and arrival).
- BLND: Blind or vision impaired passenger requiring a walker through the airport to the aircraft seat and a separate safety briefing from cabin crew.
- BDGR: Passenger travelling with a guide/assistance dog requiring a walker through the airport to the aircraft seat and a separate safety briefing from cabin crew.
- BLDP: Blind or vision impaired passenger requiring no assistance (travelling alone or accompanied).
- DPNA: Passenger with a cognitive or non-visible disability requiring assistance.

Passengers should contact the Assisted Travel Team to provide details on the type and level of assistance required. Specific codes include: 
- DEAF: Passenger is profoundly deaf.
- SVAN: Passenger travelling with a guide/assistance dog, but no special assistance services are required.
Passengers requiring in-flight therapeutic oxygen must pre-book and pay for the service at least 7 days prior to travel by contacting the Special Assistance Line (select option OXYG).
Ryanair is committed to providing appropriate seating for passengers requiring special assistance, subject to safety guidelines and available seating options.

Passengers booked under the following assistance types will be allocated a suitable on-board seat free of charge, along with one travel companion: BDGR, SVAN, WCHC, WCHS, and DPNA. For safety reasons, these passengers should be seated next to a window, unless the window seat is occupied by their travel companion or the flight load allows for empty seats between the passenger and the window.

Passengers who are blind or visually impaired should notify the airline in advance, preferably on the day of booking, even if they do not require airport assistance. This ensures that cabin crew can provide an appropriate safety demonstration.

To carry or use a special medical item onboard, passengers must submit their requirements via the online form. The special assistance team will review the request and provide written authorization if approved.

Passengers who need to include medical equipment in their checked-in luggage must contact the Special Assistance Line to obtain a 'Checked Baggage Waiver' letter. This letter must be presented to staff at the bag-drop desk.

Passengers who prefer to bring medical equipment as carry-on baggage should contact the Special Assistance Line to request a 'Cabin Baggage Waiver' letter. This letter must be shown to staff at the boarding gate.
The Special Assistance team is available via chat or phone to provide support and help ensure a smooth and comfortable journey.

A medical equipment baggage waiver letter is required to carry any medical equipment onboard. Passengers with pre-existing medical conditions who need to carry equipment in addition to their standard baggage allowance must contact the Special Assistance team in advance. A waiver letter will be issued upon provision of a doctor's letter confirming the requirements.
Only items specifically listed in the waiver letter will be accepted for carriage free of charge. These items must be packed separately and made available for inspection at the airport.

A 'Fit to Fly' form must be completed if fitness to travel is in doubt due to recent illness, hospitalization, injury, surgery, an existing unstable medical condition, or the intent to use medical equipment or therapeutic oxygen onboard. Passengers should consult a doctor before booking if they have doubts about their fitness to fly.

To add assistance to an existing Ryanair booking: 1. Log into your myRyanair account and navigate to 'My Bookings'. 2. Select 'Manage this booking' for the relevant flight. 3. Choose the 'Assisted Travel' option. 4. Select the passenger(s) requiring assistance. 5. Confirm the type of assistance for the outbound and return flights (use the toggle if different assistance is required for the return). 6. Click 'Save'. An updated email itinerary will be sent once the details are added.

Ryanair recommends requesting assistance services during the initial booking process. Assistance can be pre-booked via the website up to 48 hours before the scheduled flight departure. For requests made between 48 and 12 hours before departure, passengers must contact the Assistance team directly. If notice is not provided at least 48 hours in advance, the required assistance may be unavailable, which may disrupt travel plans.
To ensure that pre-booked Assisted Travel can be provided, passengers should present themselves at the airport assistance desk 2 hours before their scheduled flight departure time.
Various types of airport assisted travel are available. These services can be booked in advance through the Ryanair website or by contacting the Assistance Team.

Passengers travelling with an electric wheelchair or mobility scooter must select this option during the booking process. Following this selection, the airline will send an email requesting specific details regarding the mobility device.
Information regarding mobility equipment can be added after the booking is completed via the 'Manage Booking' portal or by contacting the airline through the Chat service.
When notifying the airline about a mobility device, passengers must provide the make, weight, battery type, and dimensions, including the lowest collapsed height of the wheelchair.
Passengers with reduced mobility are entitled to bring up to two pieces of mobility equipment and any necessary medical equipment on board the aircraft free of charge.

Due to space limitations, a maximum of two electric wheelchairs or mobility scooters are accepted per flight; therefore, adding equipment at the time of booking is recommended. Passengers are required to bring the device's operation instructions to the airport.
To comply with aircraft door opening dimensions, the collapsed dimensions of a wheelchair must not exceed 81cm in height, 119cm in width, and 119cm in depth.
Passengers should contact the Special Assistance team to provide specific details regarding the type and level of assistance required for their travel.

Customers identified as Blind or Vision impaired (BLND) who require a walker are provided assistance through the airport to their aircraft seat and receive a separate safety briefing from the cabin crew.

Passengers requiring any form of airport special assistance are not permitted to use emergency exit row seats. To ensure access to emergency equipment and escape routes is not obstructed, cabin crew may restrict certain seats or move passengers to different seats for safety reasons.

Ryanair aircraft seat rows are spaced 73.66 cm (29 inches) apart, with a minimum width between armrests of 39.37 cm (15.5 inches). The minimum size of plane doors is 76.2 cm x 165.1 cm (30 x 65 inches).
Passengers may be unable to board the plane if the aircraft's seat or door dimensions do not meet their specific accessibility needs.
The Boeing 737-800 has a capacity of 189 passengers. Emergency exit seats are located at 1ABC, 16A-F, and 17A-F.
Movable armrests are not available on any window seats, nor in row/seat 1ABC, 2DEF, 16A-F, and 17A-F on the Boeing 737-800.
The Boeing 737-Max 8200 has a capacity of 197 passengers. Emergency exit seats are located at 1BC, 2ABC, 17A-F, 18A-F, 28B-E, and 29A-F.
Movable armrests are not available on any window seats, nor in row/seat 1BC, 2A, 2DEF, 17A-F, 18A-F, 28B-E, 29A, 29F, 34A, 34B, 34E, and 34F on the Boeing 737-Max 8200.

Passengers can contact Assisted Travel agents via phone at +35315757852 (local rates apply) or via free chat. Phone operating hours (CET) are: Monday-Friday 9:00-22:00, Saturday 9:00-19:00, and Sunday 10:00-19:00.
Calls connected with customer support agents are recorded for training, quality purposes, and in accordance with the privacy policy. Recordings are retained for at least 90 days.
A Fit to Fly Form can be obtained by contacting the Special Assistance team.
Dialysis, CPAP, or Bipap machines may be carried in the aircraft cabin without prior notification, provided the device fits within the cabin baggage allowance.
If a medical device is carried in addition to the standard baggage allowance, the passenger must obtain a medical baggage waiver letter by contacting the Special Assistance team.
Nebulisers, ventilators, respirators, and other respiratory devices may be carried in the aircraft cabin without prior notification if the device fits within the cabin baggage allowance.
Passengers wishing to use a nebuliser, ventilator, respirator, or any other respiratory device during a flight must contact the Special Assistance line to obtain prior approval.

section: Fit to Fly Form for Onboard Use
text: To use certain medical machines onboard, passengers must complete a 'Fit to fly form,' which is sent to the passenger after they contact the Special Assistance team.
Portable Oxygen Concentrators (POC) that meet regulatory requirements are approved for use onboard the aircraft.
Certain medical equipment, such as CPAP or Portable Dialysis machines, may be carried on the aircraft but are not permitted for use onboard.
Passengers are permitted to carry syringes in the aircraft cabin for medical reasons (e.g., diabetes), provided they carry a doctor's letter confirming the requirement.
Passengers wishing to carry a defibrillator, whether as part of or in addition to their cabin baggage, must obtain a medical equipment baggage waiver letter by contacting the Special Assistance team.
All equipment is equipped with a grip bar.

Air carriers reserve the right to refuse carriage to passengers with medical conditions that may worsen or have serious consequences for themselves or other passengers during a flight. Certain conditions require medical clearance before flying. Passengers who have recently undergone surgery or suffer from specific medical conditions are recommended to consult their doctor regarding their suitability to fly.
Travel is permitted after the following recovery periods: 10 days for major chest, abdominal, cranial, or middle ear surgery, and 6 days for a tonsillectomy.
For safety reasons, Ryanair requires a non-disabled person aged 18 or older to accompany any reduced mobility passenger who is not self-reliant.

The definitions and guidelines for air passenger self-reliance are based on the UK Department for Transport's 'Access to Air Travel for Disabled People: Code of Practice (July 2008)' and the European Commission's Guidelines on the application of Regulation EC 1107/2006 (2012).
To be considered self-reliant, a passenger must meet the requirements across all specified self-reliance categories.
Travel is permitted 1 day after cataract, corneal laser, or other eye surgeries.

Passengers with chronic bronchitis, emphysema, or other forms of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) may be able to travel with therapeutic oxygen. This service must be booked in advance via the Special Assistance Call Centre or chat and is subject to a charge.
Unless otherwise advised by a doctor, passengers with epilepsy should not travel within 24 hours of a grand mal fit.
Unless otherwise advised by a doctor, passengers should not fly within 7 days of a heart attack, and only then if travel is essential.

section: Right to Refuse and Assistance Requirements
text: Airlines reserve the right to refuse carriage to passengers with conditions that may worsen or endanger themselves or others during flight. Passengers who are not self-reliant must travel with a non-disabled accompanying adult aged 18 or over who can provide the necessary assistance; otherwise, the passenger will be denied boarding.
To travel with a Portable Oxygen Concentrator (POC), passengers must contact the Special Assistance team to obtain prior approval and a Medical Equipment Approval & Baggage Waiver.

The Medical Equipment Approval & Baggage Waiver letter must be presented at the bag drop desk or the boarding gate (if traveling without checked baggage). Passengers must also carry a 'fit to fly' form to present to staff at the airport on the day of travel.

Passengers wishing to use a POC during a flight must obtain prior approval from the Special Assistance team. Once the device is approved for onboard use, the passenger must carry a doctor's letter confirming they are fit to fly.

When using a POC onboard, it is the passenger's responsibility to ensure they have sufficient fully charged batteries for the flight duration and any potential delays, as POCs and batteries cannot be charged on board.
The provision of therapeutic oxygen is restricted to one customer per flight.

Passengers requiring therapeutic oxygen should contact the Special Assistance team, ideally on the day of booking. A medical clearance form will be provided, which must be completed and returned for validation between 14 days and 2 days prior to the outbound flight.
Passengers must carry the validated Therapeutic Oxygen Medical Clearance form on all flights; travel will not be permitted without this document.
A fee per flight is applicable for the provision of therapeutic oxygen.

The fee for therapeutic oxygen is payable when the Medical Clearance Form is returned for validation. Once validated, the form is returned via email and must be presented to the cabin crew upon boarding the aircraft.
Ryanair provides therapeutic oxygen at a continuous flow rate of 2 litres per minute for a maximum duration of 250 minutes.
Ryanair cannot accept passengers for travel if they require a continuous supply of oxygen for more than 250 minutes.
Postural supports are not permitted for children under 2 years of age.
Passengers are encouraged to research approved postural supports online before booking to ensure their needs can be met. Permitted postural supports must be supplied and fitted by the passenger.
The Meru Travel Chair is permitted for children aged approximately 3 to 11 years who are unable to use standard aircraft seating. This equipment must be provided by the passenger.

The Crelling Harness HSB1 consists of shoulder straps designed to convert a standard seat belt into a supportive harness. It is suitable for passengers from 9 years of age to adults and must be provided by the passenger.
The Crelling Harness Model 27 is a full 5-point harness designed to offer additional support and is suitable for passengers from 2 years of age to adults.

Passengers using postural supports must be accompanied by an adult (over 18). For Crelling harnesses, the device must be used according to the manufacturer's instructions regarding weight, age, and installation; passengers are advised to have these instructions available for reference on board.

The Amsafe CARES (Child Aviation Restraint System) is a belt and buckle device that attaches to the aircraft seat and is used with the aircraft seatbelt. It is designed for children weighing between 22 and 44 pounds (10kg - 20kg). The restraint must be provided by the passenger, used according to manufacturer instructions, and the child must be accompanied by an adult (over 18).

The Firefly GoTo Seat Size 2 is a lightweight, portable seating solution for children aged 2-5 years who require additional postural support and stability. This equipment must be provided by the passenger.

The Burnett Body Support consists of a sealed welded casing filled with micro beads that mould to the passenger's profile when air is extracted via a valve. It is available in different sizes and must be provided by the passenger.
The approved postural supports are intended for passengers who cannot sit upright unaided and who do not meet the criteria for sitting on an adult's lap or using a car seat onboard.
Mobility equipment can be added at the time of booking after the relevant assistance requirements have been selected.

To enter EU/EEA countries, the EU Pet Passport or veterinary health certificate must be supported by documentation confirming that the guide/assistance dog is affiliated with either the International Guide Dog Federation or Assistance Dogs International (ADI).
Guide/assistance dogs must possess a pet passport. In countries that do not issue pet passports, an official veterinary health certificate is required.

It is the passenger's responsibility to ensure the Pet Passport is up to date and complies with the vaccination and treatment requirements of the destination country. Ryanair accepts no liability for guide/assistance dogs that are incorrectly documented.
Guide and assistance dog owners in countries that do not issue Pet Passports must obtain an official veterinary health certificate to demonstrate compliance with the rules of the Pet Travel Scheme.

To be accepted on a Ryanair flight, a guide/assistance dog must be trained to perform tasks for an individual with a physical, sensory, or psychiatric disability. The dog must be certified by an organization that is a full member of Assistance Dogs International (ADI), Assistance Dogs UK, or the International Guide Dog Federation (IGDF).
Emotional support or therapy dogs that are not recognized as assistance dogs by ADI, Assistance Dogs UK, or IGDF are not permitted to travel with Ryanair.
Guide/assistance dogs must wear a standard identifying jacket or harness throughout the journey. They are permitted in the aircraft cabin and must sit on the floor at the passenger's feet.
Passengers who have doubts regarding their dog's eligibility to travel are recommended to contact the destination airport prior to departure to confirm Pet Passport details.
A maximum of four guide/assistance dogs are permitted per flight. The dog, including its food and containers, is carried free of charge.

Passengers traveling with a guide/assistance dog should notify Ryanair, preferably on the day of booking, via the website or the Special Assistance team. Failure to pre-advise may result in the service being unavailable at the airport, which may prevent the passenger from traveling on their booked flight.

The owner must provide a suitable harness attached to the seat belt or seat belt buckle to effectively restrain the dog during take-off, landing, and turbulence. Any arrangement that adequately secures the assistance dog is acceptable.
Plaster or resin casts that have been fitted for more than 48 hours do not need to be split for travel.

Passengers with insulin-dependent diabetes are permitted to fly. A medical agreement is not required provided the condition is stable and the passenger can self-administer their treatment. If the passenger cannot self-administer treatment, they must be accompanied by someone who can provide the necessary assistance.
Passengers with diabetes must carry a letter from their doctor confirming the necessity of carrying syringes, needles, and medication in the aircraft cabin.

Because airport security checks are not the responsibility of Ryanair, passengers must carry a doctor's letter to justify carrying 'sharp' objects on board. Syringes, needles, and medication must be kept and then discarded after leaving the aircraft; they must not be disposed of onboard.

Passengers with nut allergies should inform the cabin crew upon boarding. The crew will then make a public announcement to other passengers and ensure that no products containing nuts are sold on board.
While Ryanair asks other passengers not to open peanut products on board, the airline cannot guarantee a completely peanut-free aircraft.

Every Ryanair aircraft is equipped with an onboard aisle wheelchair to assist passengers with reduced mobility in reaching the toilet. For health and safety reasons, crew members cannot assist with transferring passengers from their seat to the wheelchair, from the wheelchair to the toilet, or with the administration of medication.
There are three toilets on board: two located at the rear and one at the front of the aircraft.
Ryanair reserves the right to impose a charge when special declarations are made.

If mobility equipment (such as a wheelchair, mobility scooter, or walking aid) is lost, delayed, or damaged, passengers must report the incident to the Lost Property Desk at the arrival airport to obtain a Property Irregularity Report (PIR). The PIR contains a 10-character file reference used to track the equipment.

To submit a claim to Ryanair for lost, delayed, or damaged mobility equipment, a Property Irregularity Report (PIR) must first be issued at the arrival airport. Claims cannot be processed without the 10-character file reference provided on the PIR.
Guide and assistance dogs are not accepted on flights to or from Morocco or Israel.
Guide and assistance dogs are accepted on all Ryanair domestic flights and all intra-EU/EEA flights.

Passengers traveling with a guide or assistance dog into the UK or Republic of Ireland must carry either a valid EU pet passport or a third-country official veterinary health certificate, along with the required medical documentation.

For entry into the UK or Republic of Ireland, the EU pet passport or veterinary health certificate must be accompanied by documentation confirming the dog is affiliated with one of the following organizations: International Guide Dog Federation, Assistance Dogs UK, or Assistance Dogs International (ADI).

Passengers traveling with a guide or assistance dog on intra-EU/EEA flights must carry a valid pet passport or a third-country official veterinary certificate, including all required medical documentation.
Wheelchairs and mobility scooters weighing more than 150kg require pre-authorization; they will not be accepted for carriage without this approval.

Lithium-ion batteries securely attached to a mobility aid must remain installed, securely contained, and isolated to prevent accidental activation. No Watt-hour (Wh) limit applies to installed batteries. The device must be made safe by removing the key, deactivating it via joystick or isolation switch, or using another isolation mechanism (e.g., Anderson Connector or Airsafe plug).
Passengers may carry a maximum of either one spare lithium-ion battery up to 300Wh, or two spare batteries up to 160Wh each, in the cabin.

Lithium-ion batteries not securely attached to a mobility aid must be removed according to manufacturer or owner instructions. Removed batteries must be protected against short circuits (e.g., by taping exposed terminals) and placed in individual protective pouches. If an operating battery is removed and carried in the cabin, it is treated as a spare battery and is subject to the spare battery allowance (1 x 300Wh or 2 x 160Wh).

Dry cell, gel cell, or non-spillable batteries securely attached to a mobility aid must remain installed, securely contained, and isolated to prevent accidental activation. There are no Watt-hour (Wh) restrictions for these batteries when installed.
Dry cell, gel cell, or non-spillable batteries that are not securely attached to the mobility aid must be removed following the manufacturer's or device owner's instructions.
Batteries carried in the aircraft hold must be protected against short circuits (e.g., by covering or taping exposed terminals) and placed in strong, rigid packaging.
Mobility equipment is exempt from the standard 32kg single item weight limit.

section: Insurance and Liability for Mobility Aids
text: Travel insurance for mobility aids is recommended. Under the Montreal Convention 1999, airline liability is limited to 1,519 Special Drawing Rights (approximately €1,800). Customers can increase this liability to 2,262 Special Drawing Rights (approximately €2,600) by submitting a Special Declaration of Value Form.
